title: The Nyaya Sutras — The Logic of Liberation
subtitle: The 16 categories (padārtha) as a complete epistemology; pramāṇa as the instruments of discernment — compared to Tantraloka's three pramāṇas

summary.one_line: Gotama's Nyāya Sūtras analyzed as the foundational Indian epistemology, with the 16 padārthas as a complete system of inquiry and apavarga (liberation) as the cessation of suffering through right k...
summary.scope: Covers the 16 categories (padārtha), the four pramāṇas (perception, inference, comparison, testimony), the theory of inference (anumāna) as Śāktopāya, the 5-member syllogism, and the concept of apavarga as liberation through right knowledge.

sources.label: The Nyāya Sūtras of Gotama (S.C. Vidyabhusana trans., 1913)

body.text: [See NS I.i.1: 'Supreme felicity is attained by the knowledge about the true nature of sixteen categories, viz., means of right knowledge (pramāṇa), object of right knowledge (prameya), doubt (saṁśaya), purpose (prayojana), familiar instance (dṛṣṭānta), established tenet (siddhānta), members (avayava), confutation (tarka), ascertainment (nirṇaya), discussion (vāda), wrangling (jalpa), cavil (vitaṇḍā), fallacy (hetvābhāsa), quibble (chala), futility (jāti), and occasion for rebuke (nigrahasthāna).' These 16 padārthas are not mere categories but a complete noetic engine: the first two (pramāṇa and prameya) are the epistemological and ontological foundations; doubt through ascertainment are the method of inquiry; discussion through rebuke are the dialectical safeguards. Compare to the 36 tattvas of Trika: both are complete maps of reality-as-inquiry.]

[See NS I.i.3: 'Perception, inference, comparison and word (verbal testimony) — these are the means of right knowledge.' The Cārvākas admit one (perception), Vaiśeṣikas and Bauddhas admit two (perception and inference), Sāṅkhyas admit three (adding verbal testimony), and the Naiyāyikas four (adding comparison). This graded expansion of pramāṇas mirrors the graded expansion of noetic instruments in Tantraloka I.178-186, where Abhinavagupta establishes three pramāṇas (pratyakṣa, anumāna, āgama) corresponding to the three upāyas (āṇava, śākta, śāmbhava). Nyāya's four pramāṇas map to the same structure: perception = anavopāya (body-based), inference = śāktopāya (will-based), testimony = śāmbhavopāya (grace-based), comparison = the synthetic movement between them.]

[See NS I.i.5: 'Inference is knowledge which is preceded by perception, and is of three kinds, viz., a priori (pūrvavat), a posteriori (śeṣavat), and commonly seen (sāmānyato dṛṣṭa).' The threefold inference corresponds to the three movements of Śāktopāya: pūrvavat (from cause to effect) = the descending arc of emanation; śeṣavat (from effect to cause) = the ascending arc of recognition; sāmānyato dṛṣṭa (from common property) = the synthetic grasp of identity. Vātsyāyana notes that the third type reveals 'a substance called soul' through observed qualities — this is precisely the Śāktopāya method of inferring Śiva from the reflection of consciousness in the world.]

[See NS I.i.2: 'Pain, birth, activity, faults and misapprehension — on the successive annihilation of these in the reverse order, there follows release (apavarga).' And NS I.i.22: 'Release (apavarga) is the absolute deliverance from pain.' The Nyāya path is a systematic deconstruction: misapprehension (mithyā-jñāna) → faults (doṣa: rāga/dveṣa/moha) → activity (pravṛtti) → birth (janma) → pain (duḥkha). True knowledge of the 16 categories breaks the chain in reverse. This is identical in structure to the Tantric chain of mala (impurity): āṇava-mala → māyīya-mala → kārma-mala. Both systems agree: knowledge is not informational but liberative — it removes the veil of misapprehension.]

[See NS I.i.32-39. The five members of the syllogism are: proposition (pratijñā), reason (hetu), example (udāharaṇa), application (upanaya), conclusion (nigamana). E.g.: 'The hill is fiery (proposition); because it smokes (reason); whatever smokes is fiery, like a kitchen (example); so is the hill (application); therefore it is fiery (conclusion).' This is the hermeneutical engine of Nyāya — a fivefold movement that mirrors the five acts of Śiva (pañca-kṛtya): creation (proposition), preservation (reason), concealment (example), revelation (application), and grace (conclusion). The syllogism is a linguistic re-enactment of the cosmos.]

[See NS III.i.1-III.ii, establishing the existence of the soul (ātman) through inference from desire, aversion, volition, pleasure, pain, and cognition. 'The soul is established' — it is not the body, not the senses, not the mind (manas), but the substrate of consciousness. The Nyāya ātman is the cicchakti — the knower that is known only through its manifestations. Liberation (apavarga) is not the soul's destruction but its release from the cycle of pain through the removal of misapprehension. This is jīvanmukti: the soul continues in the body but without bondage, like a potter's wheel that continues spinning after the potter stops — a metaphor that appears in both Nyāya and Trika.]

gaps: Full comparison with Abhinavagupta's pramāṇa theory in Tantraloka I needed
Debate theory (vāda, jalpa, vitaṇḍā) and nigraha-sthāna need expansion

issues.title: Passages need direct quotation from primary text
issues.description: Currently referencing source synthesis. Direct sutra citations with commentaries needed.

issues.title: Missing treatment of hetvābhāsa (fallacies of reason)
issues.description: The five kinds of fallacious reasons (savyabhicāra, viruddha, prakaraṇasama, sādhyasama, kālātīta) are a key part of Nyāya method and need coverage.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. 6 passages covering the 16 padārthas, four pramāṇas, anumāna theory, apavarga, 5-member syllogism, and soul doctrine.
